2.description: Keywords in the full text of the categorized machine learning, old technology, and data management jobs Larger fonts indicate a higher word frequency. Source: Burning Glass, 2010–2018.

3.description: Job postings and labor stocks Panel (a) shows the fraction of employers hiring in each category. Panel (b) shows the stock of labor in each category, measured as a cumulated number of job postings, adjusting for filling and separation rates, as in (12). Source: Burning Glass, 2010–2018.

4.description: Average wage AI analysts earn on average $26,333 more per year than data managers and old technology analysts. Wage refers to annual, total compensation. Source: Payscale, 2015–2018.

5.description: AI intensity predicts a higher data-labor ratio The set of firms is those that hire some AI workers and some OT workers, over all months of observation. Data stocks are compiled using (15), assuming a data depreciation rate of 3%. Source: Burning Glass, Payscale, 2015–2018.

6.description: Data value and productivity Estimated value of the aggregate stock of data, in hundred-billions of current U.S. dollars (Panel (a)), and the productivity of financial analysts (Panel (b)). Productivity is the estimated values of AAI and AOT for AI and old technology analysts, as defined in Equations (1) and (2). Source: Burning Glass, Payscale, 2015–2018.
